meta.title: bene, bone

source.publication: Section 4: Roots, Prefixes, Suffixes and Foreign Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Root, words and meanings transcribed faithfully from the source PDF; a small number of well-attested real words on the same root are included where the source list was too short to form a complete family (4 to 8 members). Parts, glosses, context sentences, mentor note, attempt and construct options are the teaching layer, authored for the Language Garden (LANGUAGE_GARDEN_BIBLE §10).

meta.reviewer_notes: Batch 2: transcribed from the owner's roots (Greek and Latin) reference; 2 taught, 2 reach.

root.label: bene, bone
root.mentor_note: bene and bone both mean good, and nearly every word built on them describes kindness or benefit.

attempt.direction_prompt: Something harmful, or something good for you?

context_sentences: Regular short breaks turned out to be more beneficial to output than one long push through the afternoon.
The specialist confirmed the new diet would be beneficial, given his particular condition.
The old landlord had a benevolent habit of forgiving rent whenever a tenant fell on hard times.
Behind the strict exterior was a genuinely benevolent teacher who never once embarrassed a struggling student.
The scholarship fund had relied on a single anonymous benefactor for over a decade.
A benefactor from the town’s early years still lends her name to the library’s main reading room.

construct_options.text: Someone who generously gives money or help to a cause or person
Someone who causes harm, whether intentionally or not
Someone who benefits from another person’s generosity

context_sentences: The biopsy came back benign, and the whole family exhaled for the first time in a week.
Beneath his stern manner was a genuinely benign temperament that took new colleagues months to notice.

construct_options.text: Generous and willing to give money to a cause
Producing serious harm, especially to health
